content_markdown:
It is proposed to build a heat engine that would operate between a hot reservoir at a temperature of $400\text{ K}$ and a cold reservoir at $300\text{ K}$. See the diagram below. In each cycle it would take $100\text{ J}$ from the hot reservoir, lose $25\text{ J}$ to the cold reservoir and do $75\text{ J}$ of work.

This proposed heat engine would violate

diagram_metadata.description: A schematic diagram of a heat engine showing energy flow from a hot reservoir (400 K) to a cold reservoir (300 K). 100 J enters the engine, 75 J leaves as work out, and 25 J leaves to the cold reservoir.

options.label: A
options.text: both the first and the second laws of thermodynamics.

options.label: B
options.text: the first but not the second law of thermodynamics.

options.label: C
options.text: the second but not the first law of thermodynamics.

options.label: D
options.text: neither the first nor the second law of thermodynamics.

vibe_explanation: The first law is satisfied because energy is conserved ($100\text{ J} = 75\text{ J} + 25\text{ J}$). However, the maximum theoretical efficiency (Carnot efficiency) is $1 - \frac{T_C}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{300}{400} = 0.25$ or $25\%$. This engine claims an efficiency of $\frac{W}{Q_H} = \frac{75}{100} = 0.75$ or $75\%$. Since the proposed efficiency exceeds the Carnot efficiency, it violates the second law of thermodynamics.
